data.description:

  • The stock price return data is mixed, with a 0.8% decline over the past 7 days and a 0.6% decline over 30 days, set against gains of 1.3% year to date, 3.0% over 1 year, 41.0% over 3 years, and 48.2% over 5 years.

Approach 1: Old National Bancorp Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a bank is expected to earn on its equity above the return that shareholders require, then capitalizes those surplus earnings into an intrinsic value per share.

For Old National Bancorp, the starting point is an estimated Book Value of $20.67 per share and a Stable EPS of $2.85 per share, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 8 analysts. The model applies a Cost of Equity of $1.84 per share, which implies an Excess Return of $1.02 per share. In other words, the bank is expected to earn more on its equity than the return investors are assumed to require.

The Average Return on Equity is set at 11.70%, and analysts see Stable Book Value at $24.39 per share, based on forecasts from 10 analysts. Combining these inputs, the Excess Returns framework arrives at an intrinsic value of about $48.13 per share. Against a recent share price around $22.85, this implies the stock is treated as 52.5% undervalued under this model.

For a profitable bank like Old National Bancorp, the P/E ratio is a useful way to relate what you pay for each share to the earnings that support it. It gives you a quick feel for how the market is pricing the company’s earnings power today.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E depends on how investors view growth prospects and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually means a lower one.

Old National Bancorp currently trades on a P/E of 15.12x. That is above the Banks industry average of 11.88x and also above a peer group average of 13.60x. Approach 1: Visteon Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what those future dollars are worth in your hand right now.

For Visteon, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $373.9 million, and analysts provide explicit forecasts out to 2027, with Simply Wall St extrapolating cash flows beyond that to build a 10 year path. For example, projected free cash flow in 2026 is $224.3 million and in 2027 is $229.8 million, with later years tapering to around $160.1 million by 2035, all in dollar terms.

When all of those projected cash flows are discounted back, the estimated intrinsic value comes out at about $94.62 per share. Against the recent share price of US$98.22, the DCF implies Visteon is roughly 3.8% overvalued, which is a relatively small gap.

In simple terms, a higher P/E usually means investors are willing to pay more for each dollar of earnings, often because they expect stronger growth or see the earnings as relatively dependable.

data.title: Is Nasdaq (NDAQ) Pricing Leave Room After A 31.6% One Year Gain
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Nasdaq's current share price reflects its true value, you are not alone. This article will walk through what the numbers are actually saying about the stock.
  • Nasdaq recently closed at US$100.07, with returns of 7.7% over the last 30 days and 31.6% over the past year, while the last 7 days show a 0.3% decline.
  • These price moves sit against a backdrop of ongoing attention on Nasdaq's role as a major market operator and index provider, as investors monitor how trading activity and product demand influence sentiment toward the stock. Broader interest in exchange and market infrastructure companies also provides additional context for how the market currently views Nasdaq.
  • Despite this, Nasdaq's current valuation score sits at 0 out of 6. This means none of the six valuation checks point to the shares being undervalued right now. We will look at what each method says, then finish with a way to assess value that ties the numbers back to the bigger picture.

Nasdaq scores just 0/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Nasdaq Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company generates over and above the return that shareholders require. Instead of focusing on cash flows, it compares the return on equity to the cost of equity and capitalizes that gap into an intrinsic value per share.

For Nasdaq, the starting point is a Book Value of US$20.99 per share. Using the median return on equity from the past 5 years, the model estimates Stable EPS of US$2.43 per share, while the required Cost of Equity is US$2.01 per share. That gap of US$0.43 per share is the Excess Return, built on an Average Return on Equity of 10.30%.

The model then assumes this profitability is earned on a Stable Book Value of US$23.62 per share, based on weighted future book value estimates from 3 analysts. When these excess returns are capitalized, the Excess Returns valuation implies an intrinsic value that is 215.4% below the current share price of US$100.07. On this method, the stock screens as very expensive.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests Nasdaq may be overvalued by 215.4%. Discover 887 undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Nasdaq.

Approach 2: Nasdaq Price vs Earnings

For a profitable business like Nasdaq, the P/E ratio is a useful way to see what investors are currently willing to pay for each dollar of earnings. It ties the share price directly to the company’s bottom line, which is what ultimately supports returns over time.

What counts as a "normal" or "fair" P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk profile. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk typically comes with a lower multiple.

Nasdaq is trading on a P/E of 35.16x. That sits above the Capital Markets industry average of 25.62x and slightly above the peer average of 34.26x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Nasdaq is 16.73x, which is a proprietary estimate of what the P/E could be given factors such as earnings growth, margins, industry, market cap and specific risks.

The Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for Nasdaq’s own fundamentals instead of assuming all companies in the group deserve similar pricing. With the current P/E of 35.16x sitting well above the Fair Ratio of 16.73x, the stock screens as expensive on this metric.

Result: OVERVALUED
